title: skopeo: add `--require-signed`
body:
In bootc, we want the ability to assert that signature verification is
enforced.

Add a new top-level `--require-signed` switch. When passed, we use the
new `RequireSignatureVerification()` method to ensure that signature
verification is enforced.

Part of https://github.com/containers/skopeo/issues/1829.

comments.body: This requires https://github.com/containers/container-libs/pull/355 and still needs tests.
Ephemeral COPR build failed. @containers/packit-build please check.
@jlebon Please see @mtrmac 's question and it looks like this needs a rebase.
@TomSweeneyRedHat https://github.com/containers/container-libs/pull/355 needs to happen first, either way.
Sorry, I do intend to get back to this. Will try tomorrow, otherwise next week.
Updated for the latest version in https://github.com/containers/container-libs/pull/355 but still needs tests.
(The c/image feature is available in this repo after #2753 .)
A friendly reminder that this PR had no activity for 30 days.
This is now ready for review!

This PR came back to the forefront of my mind as I was working on something different related to signing.

So we never did anything to wire this up in bootc side right?

I hate to escalate the scope here...but the thing about a CLI option is while it was easy to implement, it doesn't have a good UX - we don't want users to always need to do `bootc upgrade --require-signed` each time. It needs a config. I think we can probably invent one bootc side, but...

One thing I could imagine here is we try to create the ability to have separate policies for "host" vs "app". Another way I'd like to do this is: "require signatures for privileged containers" - that makes the host not special at all.

Hmm wait but don't we already have `ostree-image-signed:` which is hooked to `--enforce-container-sigpolicy`? I.e. it's already a one-time thing at `switch` or `install` time and then at `upgrade` time bootc could spawn the skopeo proxy with `--require-signed` when in enforcing mode and we can drop the hacky `policy.json` parsing bits. At least that's how I was picturing it when I wrote this. Supporting this through the proxy API would be nicer though. (I meant to keep pushing all this forward on the side, but...)
